verdict.reason: Refuted as a duplicate and as overstating normative absence. D18/Task 2 explicitly restricts `init`/commit/clone commands to “this fixture builder only” and says they “are not added to the weaver-facing allowlist”; the Global constraint “Read-only weaving” separately requires weavers to invoke only allowlisted git commands. Thus a weaver using that executor would violate existing requirements even if the advisory scanner did not detect it. The claimed mechanical enforcement gap is exactly the broader defect already registered as h-34: permitted `node:child_process` access and in-process weavers allow non-allowlisted commands while the outbound scanner checks module specifiers rather than capabilities. The proposed fixture-executor import is merely one instance of that same capability gap; moreover, the plan does not require the test-only executor to be exported as an importable capability.

harvested.scope: D5 'one canonical timestamp' / header woven_at vs status records appended 'after their target edge and before close()' (Task 3 appendStatus)
harvested.claim: Every record — including human-authorized status records appended during construction of a separate ledger — carries the single captured `woven_at` of the weave. A human adjudication record therefore asserts it was made at the weave's canonical timestamp, which is false whenever adjudication happens later in the ledger-construction session, and there is no field for the actual adjudication time. Worse, verifyLedger checks 'wrong timestamp' as a tamper case, so a truthfully-timestamped status record would fail verification: the schema structurally forces human adjudications to carry a fabricated timestamp, destroying the audit meaning of when a proposal was accepted/rejected.
harvested.suggested_fix: Either state explicitly that woven_at on status records means ledger-session time (and record real adjudication time in source_ref evidence), or add a validated adjudicated_at field to status records with its own schema.

verdict.reason: This is an exact duplicate of h-373. It is also substantively refuted by the defined timestamp semantics: spec v2.8 §“Records — facts and envelopes are separate” defines `woven_at` as part of the ledger-owned “signed observation envelope” and requires one canonical timestamp per weave, not the wall-clock time of each underlying assertion or adjudication. Plan D5 likewise freezes one timestamp per weave, while §“Assertion status” says status records use “the same envelope discipline,” and Task 3 requires `appendStatus` to append with that discipline. Therefore `woven_at` on a status record identifies the immutable ledger artifact/session that enveloped the transition; it does not claim to be an `adjudicated_at` event timestamp. The wrong-timestamp verification case correctly enforces consistency with that canonical envelope timestamp rather than prohibiting a truthful adjudication timestamp promised by the schema. No requirement promises a separate adjudication-time field.

harvested.scope: Task 3 createLedger signature `{signKey?, wovenAt?, repoHead?, repositoryRef?}` vs Task 5 driver 'derives repository_ref with deriveRepositoryRef' and Task 7 'keyless full weave'
harvested.claim: createLedger accepts injectable wovenAt/repoHead/repositoryRef 'unless a test injects them', but nothing restricts injection to tests: the driver (weave.mjs) and the Task 7 run.mjs both call createLedger programmatically, and no mechanism distinguishes test callers from production callers. A driver bug or a hostile caller can inject an arbitrary repoHead or repositoryRef; verifyLedger only checks repository_ref SHAPE ('recomputes its shape') and locator agreement with the header — it never re-derives the ref against the actual repository, so a ledger woven with a spoofed repository_ref (all locators consistently carrying the spoofed value) verifies ok:true. The spec says 'validators reject locators whose repository_ref does not match the derived value', but verifyLedger, the only validator consumers run, has no repository to derive against and no requirement to try.
harvested.suggested_fix: Specify that verifyLedger optionally accepts an expectedRepositoryRef (mandatory for flagship/evidence verification), and that the driver never injects header values in production paths; add a test where a ledger with an internally consistent but wrong repository_ref is rejected when verified in the real repository.

verdict.reason: Refuted as a duplicate. Register entry h-372 already states the same production-reachable createLedger injection route and the same consequence: internally consistent spoofed repository_ref values pass verifyLedger because it checks header agreement rather than repository re-derivation. The verifier-interface portion is also independently covered by h-154 and h-281.

harvested.scope: Task 3 close(coverage) 'rejects coverage that marks any weaver skipped when an already-appended edge is asserted by that weaver' vs appendEdge with no coverage knowledge — human/model asserted_by spoofing of weaver ids is blocked, but the reverse hole exists
harvested.claim: appendEdge accepts any edge whose asserted_by/status coupling is valid at append time, and the skipped-weaver contradiction is only checked against the five weaver ids. But nothing prevents appending an edge with asserted_by = a weaver id directly through the ledger API (the coupling table REQUIRES deterministic-extraction for weaver ids, it does not restrict WHO may append). thread-ledger.mjs is an exported module; any code holding the handle — including Task 7's run.mjs or a future consumer building 'a separately constructed ledger' for human adjudication — can append edges under `clotho-git-weaver` with fabricated locators, mark that weaver executed in injected coverage, and produce a verifying ledger whose weaver-asserted facts were never produced by any weaver. verifyLedger has no way to distinguish this (h-4 covers driver-side attribution of real weaver output; this is the ledger-API-level forgery route for ledgers built outside the driver).
harvested.suggested_fix: Document that verifyLedger proves internal consistency only and that weaver-attributed facts are trustworthy only for driver-produced ledgers; or bind driver production into the trailer (e.g., a driver-only marker validated against orchestrator_refs) and specify that human-adjudication ledgers may contain only human/model asserted_by values.

verdict.reason: Exact duplicate. Register entry h-350 already identifies the same ledger-API forgery route: any holder of the exported ledger handle can append a deterministic-extraction edge under a five-weaver asserted_by id, inject coverage marking that weaver executed, and obtain a ledger that verifyLedger accepts. Task 3 and D19 validate coupling, manifest structure, and record/coverage consistency, but do not authenticate that the named weaver produced the edge; driver-side attribution controls do not cover separately constructed ledgers.

harvested.scope: Task 6 Step 1 'require ... no fatal warning' over the real repository — no specified handling for real-repository nonfatal warning growth or real duplicate headings discovered late
harvested.claim: The flagship test hard-requires zero fatal warnings over the LIVE repository, but the plan never requires a pre-flight audit that the real configured doc/contract corpus is currently free of fatal conditions (duplicate heading paths, invalid configured ledger entries, symlinked inputs). Task 4b's fixtures prove fatality on synthetic files only. If any real committed document under DOC_ROOTS already contains two identically-normalized headings (common in changelogs/templates: repeated 'Notes', 'Example'), Task 6 discovers it as an unfixable red acceptance suite — and 'fixing' it requires editing docs outside clotho/, which Task 6's PR scope ('one task per branch') and Task 7's diff review ('only clotho/, .gitignore, status/roadmap, history moves, and self-weave evidence may change') do not authorize. No task audits or sanitizes the real corpus before the acceptance gate depends on it.
harvested.suggested_fix: Add to Task 4a/4b an explicit unit that runs the splitter and ledger adapters over the real configured inventories and asserts zero fatal conditions, so corpus defects surface (and can be remediated in a scoped doc-fix PR) before Task 6 makes them acceptance blockers.

verdict.reason: Exact duplicate of h-375. It is also substantively refuted by Plan v11 Task 5 Exit, which already requires “a real-repository weave completes, verifies” before Task 6. Under D22 and the Global constraint “Fatal warnings abort,” any real duplicate heading, symlink input, invalid configured ledger entry, or other FATAL_WARNING_CODES result prevents that Task 5 exit from passing. Therefore Task 5 is the requested pre-Task-6 real-corpus preflight; Task 4b’s synthetic fixtures are not the only coverage. Nonfatal warnings are intentionally permitted and surfaced by Task 5’s stable JSON and Task 6 Step 1’s requirement to capture all nonfatal warnings, so zero nonfatal-warning growth is not an existing acceptance requirement.

harvested.scope: Header repo_head capture: 'obtain the head with exact arguments git rev-parse HEAD' — unborn/detached edge cases and the header schema '40-hex SHA' requirement
harvested.claim: The header requires repo_head to be a lowercase full 40-hex SHA, obtained via `git rev-parse HEAD`. In a repository state where HEAD is unborn (fresh clone of an empty branch) rev-parse emits 'HEAD' plus an error, and in some git versions ambiguous output; the plan defines no stable error for a non-40-hex rev-parse result at the LEDGER layer (it defines it for hash-object and rev-list but the createLedger bullet only says 'one validated repoHead'). More practically: `git rev-parse HEAD` and `deriveRepositoryRef`'s `git rev-list --max-parents=0 HEAD` are two separate git invocations with no atomicity — a concurrent checkout/commit between them yields a header whose repo_head and repository_ref were sampled from different repository states, with no detection and no ordering rule specified.
harvested.suggested_fix: Specify a stable fatal error for any non-40-hex rev-parse output, and specify the sampling order plus a post-weave re-check that repo_head is unchanged (or document the race as an accepted risk).

verdict.reason: Refuted. The unborn/detached argument does not establish a missing enforcement defect: detached HEAD resolves normally, while an unborn HEAD makes `git rev-parse HEAD` fail; Task 3 requires one validated `repoHead`, and the Header schema requires a lowercase full 40-hex SHA, so such output cannot be written into a valid header. Neither plan nor spec requires a special stable error specifically for malformed `rev-parse HEAD` output. The concurrent-HEAD portion is the same underlying defect already registered as h-33: the captured `repo_head` does not constrain later HEAD-resolving Git invocations, allowing a checkout between invocations to mix repository states. Its root-derivation example is another instance of that already-registered non-atomic HEAD sampling issue.

harvested.scope: D2/doc-weaver `docs/runs/` exclusion vs ledger-weaver run-sources under docs/runs/ vs `evidenced-by` matching in 'declared summary fields'
harvested.claim: Run summaries are matched for symbol tokens only in 'declared summary fields', but no schema for a run summary file or its declared fields is committed anywhere — unlike ledger adapters (closed adapter ids), run summaries have no adapter id, no format validation rule, and no stated behavior for a summary whose bytes are not JSON, whose declared fields are absent, or which is valid JSON with unexpected extra structure. 'validated bytes' appears twice with no validation defined. Two implementers will disagree on whether a malformed summary is a nonfatal warning (weave publishes, evidenced-by silently absent) or fatal 'invalid content address'; the flagship run-evidence group depends on exactly this undefined extraction.
harvested.suggested_fix: Commit a run-summary schema (required fields, declared evidence-field names per configured run source) in inventory.mjs with units for malformed, field-missing, and extra-structure summaries, and classify each failure as fatal or warning explicitly.

verdict.reason: Refuted as a direct duplicate. Register entry h-135 already identifies the same Task 4b defect: evidenced-by extraction depends on “declared summary fields,” while plan v11 freezes neither the run-summary schema nor the field set or matcher, allowing different edge sets for identical summary bytes. h-291 repeats the same issue. The plan’s generic statements that run-summary bytes are “validated” and processed to “adapter-validated” eligibility do not supply the missing schema or malformed/missing-field behavior, but that surviving concern is already registered as h-135.

harvested.scope: blastRadius signature/state: 'affected: nodeDescriptor[]' built 'solely from the target and verified edge endpoint descriptors' vs 'conflicting descriptors for one node id' rejection
harvested.claim: Queries reject 'conflicting descriptors for one node id', but node_id = H({kind, locator}) makes conflicting descriptors for one id cryptographically impossible if locators are honest — the check as stated can never fire on verified records (verifyLedger already recomputes ids from embedded locators). What CAN happen and is unhandled: two DIFFERENT node ids for the same logical artifact (same path/symbol, different blob_sha from a mid-weave file change per h-32, or from human-asserted edges using a stale blob). blastRadius treats them as unrelated nodes, silently splitting the inverse closure — a consumer threaded to version-A of util.mjs and a consumer threaded to version-B are never connected, and the answer under-reports affected sets with no warning, no gap, and no coverageUnknown entry. The specified impossible check gives false assurance while the real same-artifact-split hazard has no detector.
harvested.suggested_fix: Replace or supplement the conflicting-descriptor check with a same-(kind,path[,symbol])-different-content-hash detector that emits a version-split warning or gap in query results, and add a unit exercising a split node in blastRadius.

verdict.reason: Refuted. The queried identity is deliberately a content-version node, not a logical path/symbol identity. Plan D13 and spec §Core model (“Version identity, not lineage identity”) require a changed blob to mint a new node and prohibit silent reattachment; lineage exists only through explicit `supersedes`. Plan Accepted risk 9 reiterates that byte changes create new version nodes and cross-weave lineage is not inferred. Moreover, Task 5 freezes `blastRadius` to inverse `depends-on` traversal only, so it must not merge same-path nodes or traverse inferred version equivalence. Thus consumers of version A and version B being separate is the specified behavior, not under-reporting for a query whose input is the specific node id for A. Coverage-unknown is reserved by D11 for non-executed producing weavers, not for multiple legitimate artifact versions. The conflicting-descriptor rejection is harmless defensive validation of query input and need not be reachable from an honestly verified ledger to be valid. The only concrete accidental split alleged—files changing between hashing/table construction and extraction—is already registered as h-32; fixing that source snapshot/TOCTOU defect, rather than merging versions in `blastRadius`, is the appropriate remedy. Human assertions naming an older content version are also valid by design and must not be automatically conflated with the current version.

harvested.scope: Task 5 driver: 'builds one symbol and one file table' + ctx.symbols/ctx.files handed to weavers vs D26/D29 counted iterators for `package-symbols`/`package-files` — double representation of the git-weaver's sources
harvested.claim: The git-weaver's counted inventories are `package-symbols` and `package-files`, whose elements ARE the seeded descriptor tables — but ctx also carries `symbols: [...]` and `files: [...]` as plain arrays for every weaver. The plan never states whether ctx.symbols/ctx.files are the SAME collection the counted iterators wrap, whether they are present for the git-weaver at all, or whether their cardinality is required to equal the counted iterators' expected_cardinality. The code-weaver needs ctx.symbols to resolve named imports to seeded exports (it has no package-symbols iterator; its only counted id is package-modules), so seeded-symbol data must flow uncounted to at least one weaver — meaning the same data is accountable for one weaver and free for another, and the doc-weaver/test-weaver also token-match against seeded symbols with no counted or declared inventory covering that consumption. Which weavers legitimately read ctx.symbols is nowhere specified, so the D31-style 'no uncounted reads' discipline is asymmetric by construction and unverifiable.
harvested.suggested_fix: Specify per weaver exactly which ctx fields it may read, state that ctx.symbols/ctx.files are derived reference tables (not sources) exempt from counting, and for the git-weaver state that the counted iterators yield precisely these table entries with matching cardinality.

verdict.reason: Refuted as a duplicate and as overstating the accounting contract. [h-55] already identifies the same alleged defect: `ctx.symbols`/`ctx.files` and other parallel context fields coexist with `ctx.sources` counted iterators, potentially allowing counted iterators to be drained while parallel fields are read. Moreover, D26 and the normative per-weaver inventory-id table deliberately define different accountable sources per weaver: git counts seeded descriptors whose history queries complete; code counts package-module bytes; test/doc/ledger count their own test, document, ledger, run, and contract bytes. The plan does not require every derived reference-table lookup to receive an additional count. D31 expressly imposes the stronger exclusive-read rule only on contract bytes used for ledger clause resolution: solely `ctx.sources['contract-files']`, with no uncounted fallback. Task 4a also says git.mjs consumes `package-symbols` and `package-files` and then iterates each seeded symbol/file, tying those counted streams to the seeded descriptor inventories. Thus the cross-weaver difference is defined by D26/D31 rather than an independently confirmed new defect; any residual parallel-field bypass ambiguity is exactly h-55.

harvested.scope: Task 6 Step 3/expected-flagship.json: 'audited current blob SHA' for merkle-dag/obligation.mjs vs Task 5 verified manifest — no rule connects the audited target blob_sha to the woven worktree bytes
harvested.claim: Step 3 derives the target node from 'the audited current blob SHA', but if the worktree's obligation.mjs differs from the audited bytes, the weave mints edges for a DIFFERENT code-symbol node id and the flagship query returns an empty neighborhood — the test then fails with 'expectation unmatched', which is indistinguishable from a genuine weaver defect. No specified pre-check compares the audited blob_sha against `git hash-object` of the live file to produce a distinct 'expectations stale, not Clotho broken' diagnostic. (Distinct from h-6, which covers CI-staleness over time; this is the missing error-classification mechanism: the plan mandates exact content-bound expectations but provides no staleness detector, so every audited-hash mismatch presents as a false Clotho failure.)
harvested.suggested_fix: Add a Step-0 to the flagship test: for every audited content hash in expected-flagship.json, recompute the live hash and fail with a dedicated 'stale-expectation' error naming the drifted file before any weave runs.

verdict.reason: This is the same failure mode already registered in h-6: Task 6 Step 1 weaves the live worktree, while Task 6’s expectation rules and Step 3 pin the target to an audited `blob_sha` from the reviewed state; drift therefore produces an unmatched/empty target neighborhood without a dedicated stale-expectation diagnostic. Reframing that mismatch as an error-classification omission does not create a distinct defect. Moreover, Task 6 intentionally requires exact content-bound expectations and distinct matches, so failure after worktree drift is an acceptance-state failure; neither spec v2.8 nor plan v11 requires a separate diagnostic category. The proposed hash precheck would improve diagnostics, but its absence is not independently noncompliant.

harvested.scope: D21/util.mjs physical-containment helper — containment target is 'beneath the repository's real path', but the allowed write roots are `.telos/clotho/` and `docs/runs/clotho-self-weave/`; repository-beneath is too weak
harvested.claim: The containment helper requires the resolved real path to remain 'beneath the repository's real path' — not beneath the specific allowed root. Lexical validation of --out restricts to .telos/clotho/ or the self-export directory, but the physical check as specified only proves repo containment: a symlink-free path that is lexically valid yet whose ALLOWED-ROOT check is only lexical means the physical layer never re-verifies which root the path is under. Concretely, if the lexical validator has any normalization gap (e.g., accepts `.telos/clotho/../../docs/foo` rejection relies on the '..' segment rule — fine — but accepts a path where `.telos` itself was pre-created as a directory containing a non-clotho subdir via crafted casing on a case-insensitive filesystem, `.TELOS/clotho/x`), the physical check passes because the real path is still beneath the repository. The two layers check different predicates (repo-beneath vs allowed-root-beneath) and neither checks allowed-root-beneath physically; case-insensitive/Unicode-normalizing filesystems (macOS default) make byte-compared lexical paths and physical paths diverge with no specified test.
harvested.suggested_fix: Require the physical check to verify the resolved real path is beneath the resolved real path of the specific allowed root (.telos/clotho/ or the self-export dir), and add a case-folding/NFD divergence unit on the containment helper.

verdict.reason: Exact duplicate of register entry h-274: both allege that D21 physically checks only containment beneath the repository real path rather than beneath the selected allowed write root, allowing lexical/physical divergence on case-insensitive or Unicode-normalizing filesystems.

harvested.scope: Task 3 readEdges / verifyLedger line splitter — no test for oversized lines, BOM, or interior CR; 'CRLF and a missing final LF are verification errors' is asserted but the unit list omits a lone-CR and UTF-8 BOM fixture
harvested.claim: verifyLedger requires exact canonical line bytes (prev_hash hashes exact previous line bytes excluding LF), and the unit list covers CRLF and missing final LF implicitly via 'noncanonical lines' — but no listed unit covers a UTF-8 BOM prefix on the header line, a lone CR inside a line, or an unterminated final line that IS the trailer (crash during trailer write): the 'partial final line' fixture is listed for edges, but a partially-written TRAILER is the exact crash-during-close state the tail-deletion argument depends on ('a crashed weave, which verification already rejects as incomplete') and it has no dedicated fixture. If the splitter treats a partial trailer as absent-trailer rather than corrupt-line, verification still fails — but the distinction matters for the checkpoint argument and is untested.
harvested.suggested_fix: Add fixtures: BOM-prefixed header, interior lone CR, and a byte-truncated trailer line, each asserting ok:false with a stable distinct error.

verdict.reason: Refuted. The required behavior is already covered by Plan v11 Task 3 and the normative “Header, signed records, and coverage trailer” section: every line must be canonical and LF-terminated; CRLF and a missing final LF are explicitly verification errors. Task 3 requires verifyLedger to “incrementally parse exact lines” and validate canonical encoding, with units for “noncanonical lines,” “partial final line,” “missing… trailers,” and “removal of the trailer.” A BOM-prefixed header or raw/interior CR is either invalid JSON or noncanonical encoding and must fail under those requirements. A byte-truncated trailer is necessarily a partial final line; even if the splitter discards it and reports a missing trailer rather than a corrupt line, verifyLedger still returns ok:false, which is all the tail-deletion/crashed-weave argument requires. Neither the plan nor spec requires distinct stable errors for these lexical variants. “Oversized line” is also unsupported because no maximum line length or oversized-line rejection requirement exists. No concrete acceptance path to ok:true is shown.

harvested.scope: Task 4b doc-weaver target classification: 'Targets below the documentation root are doc-section, and targets below the contract root are contract-clause' vs DOC_ROOTS containing both and contract-files being a FILE list, not a root
harvested.claim: The classification rule keys node kind off 'documentation root' vs 'contract root', but inventory.mjs commits DOC_ROOTS (roots) and contract-files (an exact sorted FILE inventory) — there is no committed 'contract root'. If a contract file lives under a directory that is also a doc root (or contracts/ is simply one of the DOC_ROOTS), the doc-weaver has no specified predicate for deciding contract-clause vs doc-section: membership in the contract-files FILE list is the plausible predicate but the plan says 'below the contract root'. A contract file matched as doc-section would mint a different node id (kind participates in H({kind, locator})) than the ledger-weaver's contract-clause node for the same section, silently disconnecting documented-in facts from discharges facts at the flagship contract group.
harvested.suggested_fix: Define the classification predicate normatively: a target path is contract-clause iff it is a member of the committed contract-files inventory, otherwise doc-section; add a unit with a contract file under a shared root proving both weavers mint the same contract-clause node id.

verdict.reason: Refuted. Task 4a explicitly requires `DOC_ROOTS` to contain the reviewed “documentation and contract roots,” and Task 4b normatively classifies paths by those root roles: below a documentation root means `doc-section`; below a contract root means `contract-clause`. Thus a compliant doc-weaver cannot classify the flagship `contracts/Proposal Lifecycle.md` target as `doc-section`; doing so would directly violate Task 4b. D31’s `contract-files` list serves the ledger-weaver’s independently counted clause-resolution inventory and is not stated to replace the doc-weaver’s root-based classification. The proposed membership rule would therefore change, rather than merely clarify, the prescribed semantics. The finding also does not establish an actual overlapping documentation/contract root: the named corpus uses distinct `docs/` and `contracts/` paths. Its failure requires either an unshown overlapping configuration or an implementation that disregards the explicit Task 4b classification rule.

harvested.scope: D24/D31 skipped ledger weaver 'zero counts over required ids' vs Task 6 Step 4 doc-skip semantics — the reverse skip (--skip clotho-ledger-weaver) leaves contract-clause coverage classification undefined
harvested.claim: Coverage-unknown is reported per WEAVER for that weaver's edge kinds, but contract-clause facts are produced by two mechanisms: documented-in (doc-weaver) and discharges (ledger-weaver). The plan tests only doc-skipped; when the LEDGER weaver is skipped, expected 'discharges' gaps become coverage-unknown — fine — but documented-in edges to contract-clause targets from the doc-weaver remain, and reportGaps' 'contract' reasoning has no rule for a discharge path whose obligation endpoint kind (concern/obligation come only from ledger-weaver) is coverage-unknown while documentation of the same clause is fully covered. Neither the query spec nor units define the gap output for expectedKinds=['discharges'] with ledger-weaver skipped AND partially satisfying documented-in facts present — the only skip case exercised anywhere is the doc-weaver, so four of the five skip permutations of the coverage machinery are shipped untested.
harvested.suggested_fix: Add query units for each single-weaver skip (all five), asserting the exact coverageUnknown/gap output for the kinds each produces, and define in the plan the edge-kind→weaver ownership map those units check against.

verdict.reason: Refuted. The alleged reverse-skip ambiguity does not occur. Task 5 §Coverage awareness requires a non-executed producing weaver to yield a coverage-unknown gap instead of missing-edge, and §Direct expected kinds requires satisfaction by an outgoing edge of that exact kind whose producer executed. Therefore documented-in edges from the executed doc-weaver cannot satisfy or alter expected_kind='discharges'. With clotho-ledger-weaver skipped, the defined result is {gap:'coverage-unknown', weaver:'clotho-ledger-weaver', expected_kind:'discharges'}; any documented-in facts remain independent chain facts and can only add separately defined doc-drift gaps when currentDocs indicates drift. Task 5's D29 driver rules also explicitly define a skipped ledger-weaver as having no iterators constructed and zero counts for all three required ids. The doc-skipped acceptance case is singled out by spec §Acceptance criterion 5 and plan D31 to prove independent contract-files consumption, not because reverse skip semantics are undefined. Generic query units already require coverage-unknown for skipped producers versus missing-edge for executed producers. To the extent the finding instead objects that edge-kind-to-weaver ownership is not frozen as a closed map, that is exactly register entry h-51.

harvested.scope: Task 4a seeding: 'Duplicate descriptors are an error' vs Phase 1 export grammar over multiple package roots — same symbol name exported from two files is NOT a duplicate, but `git log -S<symbol>` is path-scoped per DESCRIPTOR while token matching (doc/test/ledger weavers) is symbol-NAME based
harvested.claim: doc-weaver, test-weaver, and ledger-weaver match 'an identifier-token match for a seeded symbol' — matching by NAME. With two seeded symbols named `validate` in different files (inevitable across many package roots), a doc mention of `validate` matches both descriptors, and the plan gives no rule: emit documented-in/motivated-by/evidenced-by edges from BOTH code-symbol nodes, from neither with a warning, or is it ambiguous-fatal? Every choice changes the flagship fact set and review set. The fixture list ('alpha'/'alphabet') tests substring safety but no fixture anywhere seeds the SAME identifier from two files. This is a determinism-relevant unspecified branch in three weavers.
harvested.suggested_fix: Specify the rule (emit an edge from every seeded symbol with that name — consistent with D3 conservatism — or emit a stable ambiguous-symbol warning and no edge), and add a duplicate-name-across-files fixture to Task 4b units.

verdict.reason: Refuted. Same-name exports in different files are distinct seeded descriptors, not duplicates: Task 4a sorts descriptors by `(path, symbol)`, and each code-symbol locator also includes `path` and `blob_sha`. Task 4b then states per matching descriptor that “an identifier-token match for a seeded symbol emits `documented-in` from the `code-symbol`”; the ledger-weaver likewise emits from a code-symbol when adapter-declared evidence names that symbol. Therefore every seeded descriptor whose symbol token matches emits its own edge. Those edges have different `from_node` values and are not collapsed by the payload identity key; canonical sorting fixes their order. This is also the conservative fan-out required by D3, “Static weavers favor visible over-threading,” with extra flagship facts sent to the review set. The test-weaver premise is additionally incorrect: its symbol matching is scoped by a static import resolving through the import’s target module, not by a repository-global symbol-name lookup. The absence of a same-name fixture may be additional test coverage, but it does not establish an unspecified runtime branch or determinism defect.

harvested.scope: Task 5 --skip and D11 record/coverage consistency: 'never appends an edge returned under the id of a skipped weaver' — but skipping the CODE weaver breaks the git-weaver's and query layer's structural assumptions without any stated interaction rule
harvested.claim: Skips are treated as independent per weaver, but weavers share the seeded symbol/file tables built by the driver, and blastRadius/why compose kinds across weavers. Nothing specifies whether seeding (hash-object over every walked file, symbol extraction) still runs when the code-weaver — or ALL weavers — are skipped: `--skip` of all five yields a ledger with a header, zero edges, and a trailer of five skipped entries; close(coverage)'s 'rejects coverage that marks any weaver skipped when an already-appended edge is asserted by that weaver' passes vacuously, publication succeeds, and an empty-but-verifying advisory artifact is published. Whether that is intended (all-skip = valid empty weave) or should be rejected (a weave that observed nothing) is unspecified, and no unit covers all-skip or majority-skip runs.
harvested.suggested_fix: State whether an all-skipped weave is publishable, and add a unit pinning the chosen behavior (either a stable rejection or a verifying empty ledger with all-skipped manifest).

verdict.reason: Exact duplicate. Register entry h-374 already covers both alleged gaps: whether driver-global symbol/file seeding runs when the code weaver or all weavers are skipped, and whether an all-skipped run may publish an empty verifying ledger. It also states the same silent-smaller-answer scenario where skipping seeding starves otherwise executed doc/test/ledger weavers while their counted iterators still balance.

harvested.scope: Task 7 snapshot publication: 'a stale prior snapshot is removed explicitly and re-created via exclusive link' vs D20 'a pre-existing destination is preserved, never replaced'
harvested.claim: Task 7 introduces an explicit remove-then-link for the committed snapshot, directly contradicting the D20 no-replace invariant and reopening the exact TOCTOU the frozen commit point was designed to close: between the explicit unlink of the stale snapshot and the linkSync of the new one, a concurrent writer can create the destination (the run then fails EEXIST — acceptable) OR the removal itself can delete a concurrently-published artifact that was NOT the stale snapshot the run inspected (remove is not compare-and-swap: nothing binds the removed inode to the previously-verified stale bytes). The plan gives the driver a frozen no-replace discipline and then licenses the evidence script to violate it with no stated staleness verification before unlink.
harvested.suggested_fix: Require the Task 7 export to verify the existing snapshot's SHA-256 matches the previously committed snapshot before unlinking (or to publish to a content-named path and update a pointer), and state that removal of an unrecognized destination is fatal.

verdict.reason: Refuted as a duplicate. Register entry h-289 states the same Task 7 defect: explicit stale-snapshot unlink followed by exclusive link contradicts D20’s preserve-existing-destination/no-replace rule, creates a crash gap, and can delete a concurrently substituted artifact because unlink is not compare-and-swap. The candidate’s inode-binding/TOCTOU detail is already expressly covered by h-289.

harvested.scope: Task 5 status filtering: 'the latest such transition in chain order wins' — no unit tests two valid human transitions targeting the SAME edge with conflicting outcomes, and reportGaps ignores status entirely
harvested.claim: The unit list covers acceptance and rejection separately but never two sequential transitions on one edge (accept then reject, reject then accept), which is the only case where 'latest wins' is observable — the load-bearing ordering rule is shipped with zero direct coverage (h-37/h-38 cover matrix absence and array-order trust; this is the missing test for the ordering rule itself even under correct ordering). Additionally, reportGaps' 'satisfied only by an outgoing edge of that kind ... whose designated producing weaver executed' never states whether an edge whose effective status is rejected/superseded still SATISFIES an expected kind: a rejected introduced-by edge is excluded from facts, but does its expected_kind produce missing-edge? The gap semantics after status filtering are undefined and untested.
harvested.suggested_fix: Add units: accept-then-reject and reject-then-accept sequences asserting the final effective status; and specify + test that expected kinds are evaluated over the post-status-filter fact set (a rejected edge yields missing-edge).

verdict.reason: Partially confirmed. Task 5 normatively requires that “the latest such transition in chain order wins,” but its unit list only generally requires “effective status resolution through human acceptance and rejection records.” It does not explicitly require two transitions targeting the same edge in both orders. An implementation using first-transition-wins or fixed status precedence could pass the enumerated single-transition cases, so direct coverage of the ordering rule is genuinely missing. This is not duplicated by h-37 (transition legality) or h-38/h-298 (trust in caller array order). However, the claimed semantic ambiguity in reportGaps is refuted: Task 5’s Status filtering section says “every query first resolves effective statuses” and default queries exclude edges whose effective status is rejected or superseded. That applies to reportGaps before its expected-kind satisfaction rule. Thus a rejected edge cannot satisfy the expected kind; with executed producer coverage it yields missing-edge, while skipped producer coverage yields coverage-unknown. A dedicated reportGaps regression test would still be useful, but no additional specification is needed.

harvested.scope: why()/reportGaps expectedKinds closed to five kinds vs blastRadius consumers group — 'consumers are incoming depends-on edges' is a flagship group with NO query that returns incoming depends-on except blastRadius at depth>=1; threadsOf is the only kind-complete API and it takes no expectedKinds
harvested.claim: The flagship 'consumers' group is validated against blastRadius output, but blastRadius returns the inverse CLOSURE — depth-3 transitive consumers — while the audited group semantics say 'incoming depends-on edges' (direct consumers). Direct incoming edges at depth 1 and the depth-3 edge union are different sets, and Step 3 builds the fact set from blastRadius.edges at depth 3: an audited direct consumer whose edge appears is fine, but the one-to-one matching plus 'audited consumer multiplicities must match' cannot distinguish an expectation intended as direct-consumer from a transitive edge, and the plan never states at which depth the consumers group is audited. (Adjacent to h-13, which covers the tests group and evidence ballooning; this is the consumers group's own depth ambiguity: the audit rule says 'incoming depends-on', the mechanism supplies depth-3 union.)
harvested.suggested_fix: State that the consumers group is audited as the depth-3 traversed depends-on edge set (or restrict matching to depth-1 incoming edges extracted via threadsOf), and make the fixture depth explicit in expected-flagship.json semantics.

verdict.reason: Refuted. The plan explicitly fixes both scopes: Task 6 Step 3 calls `blastRadius` “at depth 3” and defines the assembled fact set from `blastRadius.edges`, while the earlier Task 6 group-semantics bullet separately defines `consumers` as “incoming depends-on edges.” These are not required to be the same set: the depth-3 closure is the broader flagship neighborhood, and unmatched transitive edges are deliberately placed in the review set under Task 6’s Review Set rule and spec §Acceptance criterion 1. Moreover, an edge expectation contains exact `from_locator_match` and `to_locator_match` values. A transitive edge whose dependency endpoint is an intermediate node cannot satisfy an expectation for a direct incoming edge to the target; if its `to` endpoint is the target, it is itself a direct incoming consumer edge. One-to-one matching therefore cannot confuse the two. The multiplicity requirement applies to distinct audited consumer expectations, while additional closure edges remain explicit unexpected facts. The claimed missing depth statement and concrete false-match scenario cannot occur under the specified matching rules.

harvested.scope: D26 counted-iterator constructor: 'increments its private count only when the consumer completes consumption of that source' with `for...of` — the JS iteration protocol cannot distinguish 'consumer finished processing item N' from 'consumer requested item N+1', and the LAST source's completion is indistinguishable from loop exit
harvested.claim: Concretely beyond h-29/h-52: with the standard iterator protocol, the wrapper learns of item N's 'completion' only when next() is called again (returning N+1) or the loop ends (return()/done). For the FINAL source, a weaver that throws or fatally fails while processing it still exits the loop via return()/throw — the iterator cannot tell normal exhaustion after successful processing from abandonment during the last item unless the protocol distinguishes return() from done:true, and the plan's own rule 'a source that raises before completion is not counted' is unimplementable for the last element without a completion signal the weaver is forbidden to give (weavers 'signal neither success counts nor totals'). The unit 'a partially consumed source is not counted' cannot even be written for the terminal element under the stated interface, so the listed test obligations are unsatisfiable as specified.
harvested.suggested_fix: Specify the protocol: count element N on the subsequent next() call, and count the final element only on the iterator's natural done:true (weaver-driven exhaustion), with return()/throw() marking non-exhaustion; document that a fatal raise during the final element yields exhausted=false, and align the unit list with this exact protocol.

verdict.reason: This is an exact duplicate of h-283, which already alleges that D26/D29's counted iterator cannot observe processing completion and specifically calls out the terminal-element/abandonment ambiguity. The claimed concrete impossibility is also overstated: a wrapper can defer counting item N until the next `next()` call; after successful processing of the final item, `for...of` requests again and receives `done: true`, whereas abrupt body completion performs IteratorClose via `return()` (and explicit iterator `throw()` is separately observable). That protocol can distinguish natural exhaustion from abandonment, although plan v11 does not explicitly freeze it. Any broader inability to prove that bytes were genuinely processed is already covered by h-29/h-52 and, most directly, h-283.

harvested.scope: Task 4a code.mjs 'static relative imports with an explicit .mjs target' (knowledge-graph grammar) vs the repository's actual cross-package import precedent ('build-gate imports from breakout') and flagship consumers group
harvested.claim: The flagship consumers of deriveExecutableRef are build-gate/proposal-gate.mjs and check-registry.mjs importing from merkle-dag/obligation.mjs — cross-package RELATIVE imports (../merkle-dag/obligation.mjs). The code-weaver accepts 'static relative imports with an explicit .mjs target' resolving 'below the closed roots' — but package roots are committed per package, and the plan never states whether a relative specifier crossing from one package root into another (../merkle-dag/…) resolves ('below the closed rootS', plural, suggests yes) or which root's walk seeded the target's symbols. If merkle-dag is not among the committed package roots (it is spine — the plan says 'Inventory every current package root'; spine packages ARE package roots but nothing confirms spine packages are seeded for symbol extraction), deriveExecutableRef is never a seeded symbol at all and the ENTIRE flagship neighborhood is empty. The plan nowhere explicitly requires merkle-dag/, build-gate/ etc. to be in packageRoots and symbol-seeded, yet the acceptance test depends on it.
harvested.suggested_fix: State explicitly that packageRoots includes every tracked package including spine packages (merkle-dag, build-gate, breakout, connectors), that seeding covers them read-only, and that cross-package relative imports between committed roots resolve; add a fixture with a cross-root import.

verdict.reason: Refuted as both duplicate and contrary to explicit plan requirements. This is substantively the same finding as h-210 (and overlaps h-273/h-346). Task 4a requires: “Inventory every current package root once,” then requires seeding `ctx.symbols` from `.mjs` files “below closed package roots” and `ctx.files` from “every walked file below the closed package roots.” Spine read-only forbids modifying `merkle-dag/` and `build-gate/`; it does not exclude them from read-only inventory or seeding. Code-weaver resolution is against a real file “below the closed roots,” not below the importer’s individual package root, and Task 5 builds one repository-wide symbol table and one file table, so no per-root symbol-table ownership decision is needed. The governing spec further defines code-weaver as the dependency graph “across the packages,” and Task 6 requires the audited `build-gate` consumer edges and the `merkle-dag/obligation.mjs::deriveExecutableRef` target to match distinctly. An implementation omitting those package roots or forbidding resolution into another committed root would violate Task 4a and deterministically fail flagship acceptance; it is not a permitted empty-neighborhood scenario.

harvested.scope: Task 1 test-all.mjs 'a unit fails if an unlisted test-*.mjs file exists' — the guard lives inside the very list-driven runner it polices, and fixtures directory naming collision
harvested.claim: scripts/fixtures/ will contain miniature packages with their own test-like files (the plan mandates fixture packages, ledgers, streams); the unlisted-test guard 'fails if an unlisted test-*.mjs file exists' with no stated scope restriction to scripts/ — a fixture package legitimately containing a test-*.mjs (e.g., to exercise the test-weaver's classification of 'sorted scripts/test-*.mjs files') would trip the guard, or the guard must exclude fixtures/, which is unstated. The test-weaver fixtures REQUIRE test-classified files ('a direct test; a script-executed test target'), so the collision is guaranteed, not hypothetical; the resolution (exclude fixtures/ from the guard) changes what the guard proves.
harvested.suggested_fix: State the guard's scope as clotho/scripts/ excluding clotho/scripts/fixtures/, and note that fixture test files are exercised by test-weavers, not the runner.

verdict.reason: Refuted. Task 1 does not require a repository-wide or recursive `test-*.mjs` scan. It contrasts `check.mjs`, which explicitly “recursively enumerates every `.mjs` file below `clotho/`,” with `test-all.mjs`, which owns a committed ordered test filename list and spawns the named Clotho suite scripts. The natural guarded namespace is therefore the sibling suite entries represented by that list, not files inside `scripts/fixtures/`. Task 4b also does not guarantee a fixture named `test-*.mjs`: test targets may be classified through literal `.mjs` paths in package `check`/`test` commands, so a direct or script-executed fixture can use any filename. Finally, placing the guard in `test-all.mjs` is not circular: `npm test` always invokes `test-all.mjs`, allowing it to inspect the suite namespace before or while spawning listed tests. The claimed collision is therefore neither mandated nor concretely guaranteed by plan v11.

harvested.scope: D3/code.mjs identifier-token use detection: 'if an imported local binding occurs as an identifier token outside its import declaration' — shadowing and property-position tokens produce false depends-on edges with no stated rule
harvested.claim: The use test is purely token-presence: a local variable declaration `const alpha = 1` shadowing an imported `alpha`, an object property `{alpha: 2}`, a property access `x.alpha`, or a label all contain the identifier token outside the import declaration. The lexical scanner 'skips comments and recognizes strings' but the plan claims no scope analysis, so shadowed or property-position occurrences count as 'use', emitting depends-on edges for imports that are semantically unused — while the plan simultaneously promises 'Unused imports emit no edge.' These two normative statements conflict for any module with a shadowing declaration or same-named property: the token test says used, the unused-import rule says no edge, and no fixture covers shadowing or property-position tokens.
harvested.suggested_fix: Define 'use' normatively as token occurrence with the stated lexical limits (property/shadow positions COUNT, accepted as conservative over-threading under D3), delete or qualify 'unused imports emit no edge' to 'imports with zero token occurrences', and add shadowing/property fixtures.

verdict.reason: Exact duplicate of register entry h-330, which raises the same D3 token-presence versus “Unused imports emit no edge” claim for shadowing, property positions, and labels. Independently, the alleged contradiction is not concrete: Task 4a defines Phase 1 use operationally as an imported local binding occurring as an identifier token outside its import declaration, rather than requiring JavaScript scope or reference analysis. D3 and Accepted Risk 3 explicitly accept conservative over-threading, while Accepted Risk 5 limits the static grammar. Under that lexical definition, an import is “unused” when no qualifying token occurs; semantic non-use due to shadowing is outside the promised precision.

harvested.scope: Task 4a exit criterion 'the closure test proves the committed per-weaver inventories ... equal to their derived closures' vs weavers' inventories necessarily containing inventory.mjs, whose bytes change in Task 4b and Task 5
harvested.claim: Every weaver closure includes clotho/inventory.mjs (weavers import it or util.mjs does). implementation_refs are CONTENT addresses (file:path@blob_sha) — but the closure/inventory tests compare PATH lists, while the trailer carries blob-bound refs computed at weave time. Task 4b and Task 5 each modify inventory.mjs (extending inventories), silently changing every already-committed weaver's mechanism identity (its implementation_refs blob for inventory.mjs) without any versioning or re-audit event: the 'mechanism-bound provenance' for the git-weaver differs between a Task 4a-era and Task 5-era weave even though the git-weaver changed by zero bytes. That is arguably correct content-binding — but the committed integer version 'label' is never required to bump, and no rule states whether cross-weave comparison of implementation_refs is meaningful; more sharply, ANY task's edit to inventory.mjs invalidates all prior determinism baselines with no test distinguishing intended inventory growth from accidental mechanism drift.
harvested.suggested_fix: Document that implementation_refs identity churns with inventory.mjs edits by design, and add a unit that diffs per-weaver closure PATH sets across a PR so unintended membership changes (vs pure content changes) are review-visible.

verdict.reason: Refuted. The observed SHA churn is the specified behavior, not a defect. Plan D14 explicitly says `implementation_refs` content-bind the exact accepted module-load closure and that “Manual integer versions remain human-readable labels only and prove nothing”; the spec’s Coverage Manifest section likewise says names and manually bumped versions identify nothing because the manifest binds the mechanism bytes themselves. Since `inventory.mjs` is in each affected closure, changing its bytes must change those weavers’ `implementation_refs`, even when their entry modules are unchanged. D33 also deliberately defines provenance at whole-file closure granularity and permits shared files to appear in multiple closures. PATH equality and byte identity are separate checks: `test-closure.mjs` detects unintended closure-membership changes, while current `git hash-object` refs expose any content change. Tasks 4b, 5, and 6 re-run closure equality/current-ref derivation, so no stale Task-4a mechanism reference is published. Cross-weave inequality therefore means mechanism-closure bytes differed, not that the integer label or entry module necessarily changed; no requirement promises semantic equivalence across such unequal refs.

harvested.scope: Task 5 test-advisory inbound scan: 'inventories package roots from tracked package.json files and scans every tracked JavaScript or TypeScript-family source file assigned to a package' — untracked files, files outside any package, and non-JS entry vectors are unscanned
harvested.claim: The inbound advisory proof scans only TRACKED files ASSIGNED TO A PACKAGE. Three inbound routes bypass it by construction: (1) a tracked JS file at repository root or in docs/ (not 'assigned to a package') importing clotho/ is never scanned; (2) any package.json script line outside clotho (e.g., "test": "node ../clotho/weave.mjs") executes Clotho without any import statement — the scanner checks module specifiers in source files, not package.json script commands, so the 'no gate/lifecycle decision reads Clotho data' guarantee has an unscanned command-invocation route; (3) GitHub workflow YAML can invoke node clotho/weave.mjs directly. seed-1 covers loader-evasion in scanned files; this is scope exclusion — whole file classes and invocation-by-command are outside the scan universe entirely.
harvested.suggested_fix: Extend the inbound scan to all tracked JS/TS files regardless of package assignment, and add a textual check over tracked package.json scripts and workflow files rejecting commands that reference clotho/ paths outside the permitted CI matrix entry.

harvested.scope: Task 3 'close is idempotent only after a successful first close' vs D22 'abort() after successful close() is a no-op' vs 'A close without valid coverage data throws and poisons the ledger'
harvested.claim: Close-failure poisoning and close idempotence interact incoherently: a first close() that throws (invalid coverage) POISONS the ledger permanently — but the driver-level flow validates coverage BEFORE close, and Task 3's own tests call close with invalid coverage expecting a throw. After such a throw, the descriptor is closed and the ledger poisoned; yet the temporary file at that point contains a header and valid edges with NO trailer — verifyLedger rejects it as incomplete (good) — but the plan says 'close idempotent only after a successful first close', implying a FAILED close may be retried? No: poisoning forbids retry. So a coverage-shape mistake in the driver is unrecoverable-by-construction even when the correct coverage object is one retry away, and nothing states this is intended; simultaneously the sentence about idempotence 'only after a successful first close' has no referent behavior for the failed-first-close case other than the poison rule two bullets later, forcing the reader to reconcile them.
harvested.suggested_fix: State explicitly: any close() failure poisons permanently, no retry, the caller must abort and remove; delete or reword the 'idempotent only after a successful first close' sentence to 'a second close() after success is a no-op; a close() after failure throws'.

The test asserts a repository state property, not a Clotho property, and unlike the blob-sha staleness (h-6) this one is presented as a 'freshness check' that will be permanently red after the first post-audit doc edit, with re-audit of expected-flagship.json as the only fix and no protocol for it.
harvested.suggested_fix: Assert instead that drift gaps are exactly the expected set (empty at audit time) OR that the drift mechanism fires correctly on a synthetically edited copy; define a re-audit protocol for expected-flagship.json when governed docs change.

harvested.scope: Task 2 units 'canonical key-order independence; array-order sensitivity; non-JSON values' — canonicalJson lacks units for the string-encoding hazards that change hashes silently (lone surrogates, non-NFC keys)
harvested.claim: deriveNodeId hashes UTF-8 bytes of canonicalJson output, and heading normalization applies NFC to heading TEXT — but canonicalJson itself has no stated rule for lone surrogates in strings (JSON.stringify emits escaped lone surrogates on modern Node but raw ill-formed UTF-16 becomes replacement-character-or-throw depending on the UTF-8 encoder), nor for whether string values are escaped (\uXXXX) or emitted raw — two encoders produce different bytes for the same logical value, changing every node id. Markdown files in the wild contain unpaired-surrogate-producing sequences only rarely, but heading text with combining characters NOT normalized (locator heading_path is NFC-normalized, good — but doc/contract PATHS and symbol strings are not NFC'd, and macOS filesystems return NFD paths from walks: the same file yields different path bytes on macOS vs Linux, minting platform-divergent node ids). harvested.suggested_fix: Freeze canonicalJson's string escaping rule, NFC-normalize (or reject non-NFC) repository paths at walk time, and add a unit with a composed/decomposed filename pair.

harvested.claim: The verifier has no explicit test or algorithm requiring decoded signatures to round-trip to the exact canonical base64 spelling. Node's base64 decoder accepts alternatives such as omitted padding; changing the final trailer signature from padded to unpadded base64 changes ledger bytes but yields the same raw Ed25519 signature. Because the trailer has no successor link and its own `record_hash` excludes `signature`, a permissive verifier can report `ok: true` after this byte tamper.
harvested.scope: Task 3 canonical signature validation and trailer tamper tests
harvested.suggested_fix: Decode and re-encode every public key and signature and require byte-for-byte equality with one frozen padded base64 form; add an independently signed fixture using valid but noncanonical base64, especially on the final trailer.

harvested.scope: Task 4b test-weaver `test-files` counted inventory ('classified test files') vs Task 4a driver walk — clotho's OWN test scripts are `scripts/test-*.mjs` files inside a committed package root, and they import thread-ledger.mjs/weave internals
harvested.claim: If clotho/ is a committed package root (required for self-weave meaningfulness, cf. unresolved h-146), the test-weaver classifies clotho/scripts/test-*.mjs as test files and emits verified-by edges — but those test files import Clotho's own modules, and the flagship weave (Step 1) then runs the test-weaver over test files that also appear in the ORCHESTRATOR/weaver implementation closures? No — test scripts are not in the closures (not imported by weavers) — the genuine defect: clotho/package.json's test command is `check && test-all`, and test-all.mjs SPAWNS test files rather than importing them, so command-inferred verified-by extraction over clotho/package.json sees only scripts/check.mjs and scripts/test-all.mjs as literal .mjs paths; the actual test files are referenced only inside test-all.mjs's committed filename LIST, which no weaver parses. Every clotho test file therefore gets test-file-classified verified-by edges (from imports) but the package-command provenance chain (D25) can never attribute clotho's own tests to its test command — meaning the self-weave's verified-by facts for Clotho's own code systematically use only one of the two provenance cases, and the runner-indirection pattern (test-all.mjs spawning listed files) is the repository-wide convention for EVERY package, so command-inferred verified-by edges are near-universally absent across the whole weave while the plan presents command inference as a load-bearing extraction path. No warning classifies 'command references a runner script, not tests' (adjacent to h-18/h-41 but specific: the repository's own universal test-runner convention structurally defeats the command-inference design, and no fixture models the test-all.mjs spawn-list pattern).
harvested.suggested_fix: Either extend the test-weaver to parse the committed test-runner list pattern (a closed grammar for test-all.mjs-style filename arrays) or explicitly document that command-inferred verified-by covers only directly-referenced paths and add a nonfatal warning code for runner-indirection, plus a fixture modeling the spawn-list convention.

harvested.scope: D13/D9 content-bound locators vs git-weaver introduced-by TARGET commit locator — a weave over a dirty worktree emits edges FROM worktree-blob-identified version nodes TO commits, but the endpoint matrix and locator validation permit an introduced-by edge whose from-node blob_sha exists in NO commit, making the edge semantically unanchored while structurally valid

No validation can catch this (verifyLedger has no repository), no warning is specified for 'from-locator blob not reachable from repo_head', and the flagship Step 3 audit hides it because audited blob SHAs match the (presumably clean) reviewed state. A dirty-worktree weave — explicitly supported per the plan's 'a changed worktree remains content-addressed' — thus systematically produces introduced-by facts that are false for every modified file, with deterministic-extraction status and no classifiable caveat. The plan should either mark such edges with a warning (blob-not-in-history) or emit no introduced-by edge for worktree-only blobs; currently neither is specified.
harvested.suggested_fix: Specify that the git-weaver (or driver) checks whether each seeded blob_sha is contained in the commit named by the introduced-by result (e.g., git cat-file existence + tree membership, or compare hash-object against HEAD blob) and, on mismatch, emits a nonfatal 'worktree-modified-source' warning and no introduced-by edge for that descriptor.

harvested.scope: Task 4a commits per-weaver implementation inventories for git+code weavers INCLUDING inventory.mjs itself in each closure — but inventory.mjs cannot contain its own blob SHA, and the closure includes inventory.mjs whose bytes include the inventory lists being validated (self-referential fixpoint at COMMIT time, not weave time)
harvested.claim: Each weaver closure includes clotho/inventory.mjs (weavers/util.mjs imports it). The committed implementation-file inventory (a PATH list inside inventory.mjs) must equal the derived closure — fine, paths only. But `inventories_consumed` carries `source_ref = file:clotho/inventory.mjs@<blob_sha>` AND implementation_refs also content-address inventory.mjs; both are computed at weave time — no commit-time self-reference. The real defect is narrower: the closure test derives closures by PARSING inventory.mjs (it is a closure member), and inventory.mjs per the file table also holds FATAL_WARNING_CODES, adapter ids, DOC_ROOTS etc. — Task 4a's exit criterion requires the closure test green, but inventory.mjs at Task 4a must already import-or-declare entries for weavers that don't exist yet? No — D17 says per-weaver lists land per task. The residual genuine issue: the frozen per-weaver required inventory-id TABLE 'committed in inventory.mjs' with 'a unit asserting that equality' must at Task 4a contain rows for all five weavers (the table is frozen as a whole) while D17 forbids naming nonexistent files — the TABLE names inventory IDS not files, so committing all five rows is legal, but the plan never says whether Task 4a commits the full five-row table or only two rows; a two-row commit breaks the 'exactly equal to the frozen normative table' unit, a five-row commit means Task 4a's inventory declares required IDs (`contract-files`, `test-files`...) whose backing source inventories (contract-files list, run sources) are NOT yet committed — leaving inventory.mjs internally inconsistent (required-ID table references inventory ids with no committed member lists) for two merge windows with no stated validity rule.
harvested.suggested_fix: State explicitly that Task 4a commits the complete five-row required-inventory-id table (IDs are names, not files, so D17 permits it) together with placeholder-free source inventories only for git/code weavers, and that the table-equality unit tolerates absent member lists for not-yet-landed weavers until Task 4b.

harvested.scope: Task 4b doc-weaver 'The whole Markdown file blob is the source ref' vs D2 section-level identity — documented-in edges' source_ref granularity makes doc-drift and re-audit UNDETECTABLE at the evidence layer for section moves within a file
harvested.claim: documented-in edges point TO a section-version node (heading_path + text_sha256) but carry source_ref = whole-file blob. If a file is edited such that a section's bytes are unchanged but its POSITION/level changes (moved under a different parent heading), heading_path changes → new node id; the old edge remains a fact about a section address that no longer exists in the current file, and currentDocs drift detection keys on docAddressKey({path, heading_path}) — 'a missing key' emits doc-drift, good. The unreported gap: a section moved WITHOUT heading_path change (sibling reorder) keeps key and text_sha256 identical while the whole-file blob (source_ref) changed — no drift is reported (correct, section unchanged) but the edge's source_ref now names bytes that no longer exist in the worktree, and no query, gap, or freshness check ever validates source_ref liveness — so 'the evidence for the assertion' becomes a dangling content address invisibly. Meanwhile flagship expectations pin exact source_ref values ('File and ledger source refs are exact content addresses from the reviewed repository state'), so ANY whole-file edit — even to an unrelated section — invalidates every documented-in expectation for that file (source_ref = whole-file blob changes), making the flagship documentation group maximally brittle: the expectation granularity (file blob) is coarser than the fact granularity (section), guaranteeing spurious expectation churn.
harvested.suggested_fix: Consider section-granular source refs for documented-in (or at minimum document that documentation expectations must be re-audited on ANY byte change to the containing file, and exclude source_ref from documentation-group matching or provide a documented re-audit protocol).

harvested.scope: Task 4a git-weaver 'emits one ... edge for the first result' + 'Every output line is validated as a full SHA' vs `git log -S<symbol> --format=%H --reverse -- <path>` output volume — validating EVERY line requires consuming full history output for every symbol even though only the first line is used, and execFileSync buffers it all (interacts with h-193 maxBuffer) but the SEMANTIC defect is unlisted: validation of all lines makes ANY future git porcelain change (warnings on stderr merged? progress lines?) or a commit with unusual encoding fatal for the whole weave
harvested.claim: 'Every output line is validated as a full SHA' converts the weave's fatality surface to the entire history of every path: one malformed line ANYWHERE in a symbol's full pickaxe output (e.g., git emitting an advice/warning line to stdout under some configs, or locale-affected output) aborts the whole weave even though only the first line matters. The rule maximizes fragility with no benefit (the used datum is line one), and no unit tests git output containing trailing empty lines or a final newline variation — the exact parsing details (split on LF, ignore trailing empty string?) are unspecified, so 'exactly one full 40-hex output line' style strictness from deriveRepositoryRef is ambiguously extended here. A repository whose history triggers any nonstandard stdout line becomes permanently unweavable.
harvested.suggested_fix: Specify tolerant-but-bounded parsing: require the FIRST line to be a full SHA (fatal otherwise), validate subsequent lines with a nonfatal warning on anomaly, and pin trailing-newline handling with a unit.

harvested.claim: The first-result rule for `git log --reverse` is not deterministic on a branched history. Two incomparable commits can independently add the same symbol or path, and commits can have equal timestamps; Git does not define a canonical order between such commits, and traversal order can vary with Git version or object packing. Selecting stdout line one can therefore emit different `introduced-by` edges from full clones containing identical commits.
harvested.scope: Task 4a git.mjs path-scoped `git log -S` and file-history commands
harvested.suggested_fix: Collect and validate all candidate SHAs, derive ancestry-minimal candidates, and fail or warn on multiple incomparable introductions; otherwise select using a frozen deterministic rule. Add a real-Git fixture with equal-timestamp introductions on merged branches.

harvested.claim: D33 requires scanner and closure derivation to share extension rules but never freezes them. harvested.scope: Task 5 verifyLedger before publish vs temporary file still writable (inode mutate)
harvested.claim: Beyond path-level TOCTOU, hard-link publication means any write to the temp path after verify mutates the would-be published inode. Without immutable chmod or re-hash from fd after link, the D20 claim that the published artifact is the verified artifact is not true at execution time under concurrent writers.
harvested.suggested_fix: chmod 0444 after final write, re-hash after linkSync via the dest path, and compare to pre-link digest before reporting published.

harvested.scope: Task 4a Phase 1 export grammar omits common export forms used in real packages
harvested.claim: Export seeding recognizes only export function/async function/const/class. Real modules using export let/var, export { name }, export default, or generators produce no seeded symbols; consumers then fall back to file-level or miss symbol threads. Accepted risk 5 acknowledges unsupported exports, but the flagship and D9 file-first-class design still depend on adequate seeding density—under-seeding is silent with only generic unsupported warnings, and no inventory of which TELOS exports the grammar actually captures is required before acceptance.
harvested.suggested_fix: Run a pre-flagship seed coverage report over packageRoots and require Eye sign-off that all flagship symbols are grammar-covered; extend grammar if not.

harvested.scope: Task 0 human workflow review gate vs numbered task order Task 1→0→2…
harvested.claim: Execution order is Task 1, then Task 0 (unbounded human workflow review), then 2–7. Between Task 1 and Task 0 merge, clotho exists without CI (known), and Task 2+ exit criteria say npm test green without Task 0 defining the clotho matrix environment yet. Local shallow clones can fail flagship/deriveRepositoryRef as test failures without a distinct environment class—serialization through an unbounded human gate with out-of-order numbers leaves environment contracts unenforced for early feature tasks.
harvested.suggested_fix: Merge Task 0 immediately after Task 1 as hard prerequisite in CI required checks, and make shallow/env failures a distinct non-product error in Task 2+ tests.
